instruction: Determine the domain and range of the function $\frac{1}{2-\sqrt{x}}$.
Are/were there any oral histories known to exist that made mention of the Ice Age or anything related to the ice age, such as now extinct animals?
response: [This article](_URL_0_) describes research that claims to identify Australian oral tradition that describes Ice-Age lower coastlines. It is an interesting thesis, and the evidence is persuasive - in my opinion. That is the only example of recorded oral tradition recounting an Ice-Age environment that I know of. Of course, in Australia it is without the ice! All that we are hoping to see is the indirect effect of the Ice age, namely, the lower sea levels.

instruction: Given \( y = (x_1, \ldots, x_N) \) and \( F(y, x_1, \ldots, x_N) = 0 \), find \(\frac{dy}{dx}\) in terms of the partial derivatives of \( F \).
Boxer Jacob ‘Baby Jake’ Matlala was born in which country?

Given the symmetric matrices \( A \) and \( B \):

\[ A = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 & 4 & 4 & 4 & 4 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 & 6 & 6 & 6 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 & 8 & 8 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 & 10 & 10 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 & 8 & 10 & 12 \end{bmatrix} \]

\[ B = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \]

If the eigenvalues of \( A \) are \( \{\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_6\} \), how can we determine which particular eigenvalue will increase by 1 when considering the matrix \( A + B \)?
response: \text{No eigenvalue of }A\text{ is increased by exactly }1\text{ in }A+B.

instruction:
Find a basis $\mathbb{B}$ for the vector space $P_2$ such that the coordinate vector $[P_2]_\mathbb{B}$ is given by
\[
\begin{bmatrix} 
p(0) \\ p(1) \\ p(2) 
\end{bmatrix}
\]
where $p(x) = a + bx + cx^2$ is a general polynomial in $P_2$.
Calculate the probability \( P\{W_t \leq a\sqrt{t} \quad \forall{t} \leq T\} \) where \( a, T > 0 \) and \( W_t \) is a Brownian motion.

Given the dynamics governed by the recursive relationship \( y(t+1) = y(t) \cdot e^{-x(t)} \) with \( y(0) = y_0 \), and the constraint \( \sum_{i=0}^{T-1} x_i = c \) for some constant \( c \), find the sequence \( [x_0, ..., x_{T-1}] \) that minimizes \( y(T) \) for a given maximum time \( T \geq 1 \).
response: Any sequence \( [x_0, ..., x_{T-1}] \) such that \( \sum_{i=0}^{T-1} x_i = c \)

instruction: Describe the pairs $(\omega_1, \omega_2)$ for which $g_2 = 0$, where $g_2 = \sum_{0\neq \omega \in L} \frac{60}{\omega^4}$ and $L = \{m\omega_1 + n\omega_2\}$.
response: \((\omega_1, \omega_2) = (s(a\tau + b), s(c\tau + d))\) where \(a, b, c, d \in \mathbb{Z}\), \(ad - bc = 1\), and \(s \in \mathbb{C}^*\), with \(\tau = e^{2i\pi / 3}\).

The temperature in space is about 3K, however there are almost no atoms in space. My understanding is that heat is essentially atomic vibrations. If there are almost no atoms, how can there be residual heat?
response: Heat can take a lot of forms. In the case of matter, it's the kinetic energy of the particles. In space, the temperature is determined by the energy density of photons.  Put another way, if you put a block of matter in space and let it cool by radiating photons it will eventually find equilibrium at about 2.7 K. It will absorb energy from photons from the cosmic microwave background at the same rate that it emits photons, thus reaching equilibrium.

instruction: difference between vietnam and india
response: India are much more typical than are the trends in Vietnam. And the early focus, which was a very appropriate one at the time, was getting children into school in the first place, which meant basically looking at resources and facilities, setting up schools, training teachers and so on.t the same time the education system in Vietnam is relatively equitable and this means that poorer children really get the same deal as those who are better-off than they are. And they are therefore not less advantaged in the school system.

Given a set \( A \) with \( |A| = n = 9k \) that is divided into three disjoint subsets \( A_1, A_2, A_3 \) each of size \( \frac{n}{3} \), and a fixed subset \( B \subset A \) with \( |B| = \frac{n}{3} \), find the probability that \( |B \cap A_i| = \frac{n}{9} \) for \( i = 1, 2, 3 \).
response: \(\frac {{\binom {n- \frac n 9} {\frac n 3 - \frac n 9} } {\binom {\frac 2 3 n - \frac {2n} 9} {\frac n 3 - \frac n 9}}} {{\binom {n} {\frac n 3} } {\binom {\frac 2 3 n} {\frac n 3}}}\)
